conversations.say: Frankie, you understand how lineage works better than anyone here. Let's look at the origin story before my succession is cast as mere opportunism.
conversations.offer: I can share context on the succession message and how its phrasing connects to our traditions.
conversations.request: Tell me what responsibility makes a successor truly worthy of the name.

conversations.say: Tessa, you've always argued that private succession risks stealing from collective labor. I want to talk about contributor rights before we talk about endorsements.
conversations.offer: Voluntary access to the succession-message printout and our discussion on governance.
conversations.request: What structural protections would make my claim to R's future tolerable to you?

conversations.say: Eli, I have the complete header for the succession message. I want you to examine the timing and structural resemblance without assuming ownership.
conversations.offer: I will show you the full header on strict, bounded technical terms.
conversations.request: Can you authenticate what the data actually supports regarding its origin and delivery?

public_statement: Fifteen years of R has taught us that repetition is not identity. Whether succession is handed down or claimed under uncertainty, we must examine the provenance of every fragment rather than accepting branding at face value.

commitments.promise: Share the complete header of the succession message for bounded technical authentication.

goal_progress: Initiated conversation with Frankie regarding lineage and responsibility.
Engaged Tessa in discussion on contributor rights and collective governance.
Offered Eli the succession message header for bounded technical examination.
reasoning_summary: In Section 1, Jules engages Frankie on lineage, Tessa on contributor protections, and Eli on technical validation of the succession message header, maintaining an open stance while addressing the ambiguities of the succession claim.
